content:
# Leviticus Chapter 21 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70v–71r
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 107–108
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_021.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 21 applies the holiness logic of the Holiness Code (chapters 17–26) inward to the priesthood itself, superimposing on the kohanim a graduated tier of obligations that exceeds those of ordinary Israelites. The chapter moves through three sections: (1) ordinary priests — restrictions on corpse-contact and mourning (vv. 1–6); (2) ordinary priests — marriage restrictions (vv. 7–9); (3) the high priest — heightened mourning and marriage rules (vv. 10–15); (4) the blemish law — physical conditions barring altar service (vv. 16–24). The governing theological logic is the camp's graduated holiness-map: the nearer to the divine presence, the stricter the demand for wholeness and separation. The chapter's two key term-clusters are q-d-sh (holiness/consecration) and ch-l-l (profanation), set in relentless dialectic throughout.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" (v. 24) | ha-kohen ha-gadol = "the high priest" | mum = "blemish" | nefesh (corpse context) = "dead person" | nezer = "crown" | parokhet = "the veil" | she'ero = "his nearest kin" | betulah = "virgin" | zonah = "prostitute" | chalalah = "a woman who has been defiled" | gerushah = "a divorced woman" | almanah = "a widow" | ishei YHWH = "the LORD's food offerings" | lechem Elohav = "the bread of his God" | English name forms throughout

---

## Consensus Translation

**21:1** And the LORD said to Moses: "Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them: No one shall make himself unclean for a dead person among his people,

**21:2** except for his nearest kin who is near to him: his mother, his father, his son, his daughter, his brother,

**21:3** and his virgin sister who is near to him, who has had no husband — for her he may make himself unclean.

**21:4** He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself.

**21:5** They shall not make bald patches on their heads, nor shave off the edges of their beards, nor make any cuts on their body.

**21:6** They shall be holy to their God and shall not profane the name of their God; for it is they who present the LORD's food offerings, the bread of their God; therefore they shall be holy.

**21:7** A woman who is a prostitute or who has been defiled they shall not marry, nor shall they marry a woman divorced from her husband, for the priest is holy to his God.

**21:8** You shall consecrate him, therefore, for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you, for I the LORD, who sanctifies you, am holy.

**21:9** And if the daughter of any priest profanes herself by going whoring, she profanes her father — she shall be burned with fire.

**21:10** "And the high priest among his brothers, on whose head the anointing oil has been poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose nor tear his garments.

**21:11** He shall not go in to any dead body, nor make himself unclean even for his father or for his mother.

**21:12** He shall not go out of the sanctuary, lest he profane the sanctuary of his God, for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him. I am the LORD.

**21:13** And he shall take a wife in her virginity.

**21:14** A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute — these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people,

**21:15** so that he may not profane his offspring among his people, for I am the LORD who sanctifies him."

**21:16** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**21:17** "Speak to Aaron, saying: None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish may approach to offer the bread of his God.

**21:18** For no one who has a blemish shall draw near: a man blind or lame, one who has a disfigured face or a limb too long,

**21:19** a man who has a broken foot or a broken hand,

**21:20** or a hunchback or a dwarf or a man with a defect in his eye or with an itching disease or scabs or crushed testicles.

**21:21** No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a blemish shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings; since he has a blemish, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God.

**21:22** He may eat the bread of his God, both the most holy things and the holy things.

**21:23** But he shall not go in to the veil or come near to the altar, because he has a blemish, that he may not profane my sanctuaries, for I am the LORD who sanctifies them."

**21:24** And Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the sons of Israel.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula / "Moses" vs. "Moshe."** EMET: "Moshe." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — English name form per project convention established at Genesis and maintained throughout. The same ruling applies to "Aaron" (EMET: "Aharon") in vv. 1, 17, 21, 24 and "Israel" (EMET: "Yisra'el") in v. 24.

**V. 1 — "Dead person" for nefesh.** EMET: "a dead person [le-nefesh]." LOGOS: "a dead person." PNEUMA: "a corpse." Consensus: "a dead person" — EMET/LOGOS. The terminology ruling specifies nefesh in the corpse context = "dead person." PNEUMA's "corpse" is a narrower, more physical term that loses the personal/relational sense of nefesh (a dead person, a soul that has died) which the Hebrew preserves. LOGOS and EMET agree on "dead person."

**V. 2 — "Nearest kin" vs. "near kin" vs. "closest relatives."** EMET: "his near kin [she'ero — lit. his flesh]." LOGOS: "his nearest kin." PNEUMA: "his closest relatives." Consensus: "his nearest kin" — LOGOS. The MT has *lish'ero ha-qarov eilav* ("for his flesh that is near to him"), with the superlative implied by the phrase. "Nearest kin" captures both the flesh-closeness of *she'er* and the proximity of *ha-qarov eilav*. EMET's "near kin" is slightly less emphatic; PNEUMA's "closest relatives" is equivalent but breaks from the LOGOS/EMET terminological pattern. The formula "who is near to him" is preserved from the MT (*ha-qarov eilav*) following EMET's more literal structure.

**V. 3 — "Who has had no husband" vs. "who has not belonged to a man."** EMET: "who has not belonged to a man [asher lo haytah le-ish]." LOGOS: "who has had no husband." PNEUMA: "still dependent on him because she has no husband." Consensus: "who has had no husband" — LOGOS. The MT's *asher lo haytah le-ish* ("who has not been/belonged to a man") is an idiom for being unmarried/not yet given in marriage. LOGOS renders this idiomatically and accurately. EMET's "not belonged to a man" is more literal but slightly awkward in English. PNEUMA's interpretive expansion ("still dependent on him because") adds explanation not present in the Hebrew.

**V. 4 — "As a husband among his people and so profane himself."** EMET: "as a husband [ba'al] among his people, to profane himself." LOGOS: "as a husband among his people and so profane himself." PNEUMA: "for those related to him only by marriage, and so defile himself." Consensus: "as a husband among his people and so profane himself" — LOGOS. This verse is the most interpretively contested in the chapter. Consensus adopts the reading that takes *ba'al* as "husband" — the priest may not extend the permitted mourning defilements to in-laws or a wife not within the listed she'er. LOGOS's rendering is the clearest English expression of this reading. PNEUMA's paraphrase ("related to him only by marriage") is interpretively correct but adds words not in the MT. EMET's "to profane himself" (infinitive construct) and LOGOS's "and so profane himself" are equivalent; LOGOS's coordinating conjunction flows better in English. Note: "profane himself" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. PNEUMA's "defile himself" — the root is ch-l-l (profane), not t-m-' (defile); "profane" is correct.

**V. 5 — "Bald patches" vs. "a baldness."** EMET: "make a baldness [qorchah] upon their head." LOGOS: "make bald patches on their heads." PNEUMA: "shave their heads bald." Consensus: "make bald patches on their heads" — LOGOS. LOGOS's "bald patches" captures the localized nature of qorchah (a mourning-baldness shaved or cut in a specific area of the head, not total shaving). EMET's "a baldness" is singular and slightly less precise; PNEUMA's "shave their heads bald" describes complete shaving, which goes beyond the sense of qorchah and conflates it with the beard-shaving prohibition.

**V. 5 — "Edges of their beards" vs. "corner of their beard."** EMET: "the corner of their beard [pe'at zekanam]." LOGOS: "the edges of their beards." PNEUMA: "the edges of their beards." Consensus: "the edges of their beards" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. *Pe'at* (corner/edge) + *zeqanam* (their beard). LOGOS's "edges" is slightly more natural than EMET's "corner" for *pe'ah* in a beard context. The plural "beards" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) correctly reflects the plural subject (the priests collectively).

**V. 5 — "Cuts on their body" vs. "a cut in their flesh."** EMET: "in their flesh they shall not cut a cut [lo yisretu saravet]." LOGOS: "make any cuts on their body." PNEUMA: "cut their bodies." Consensus: "make any cuts on their body" — LOGOS. LOGOS preserves the plural implication of *saravet* (incisions/cuts) and uses "body" rather than the more literal "flesh" (EMET) or PNEUMA's plain "cut their bodies." The infinitive-absolute construction (lit. "they shall not cut a cut") is captured by "make any cuts" in LOGOS.

**V. 6 — "Shall not profane" / "not profane."** EMET: "they shall not profane [lo yechalelu] the name of their God." LOGOS: "not profane the name of their God." PNEUMA: "must not profane the name of their God." Consensus: retains "shall not profane" — EMET/LOGOS modal form, consistent with the legal register of the chapter. PNEUMA's "must not" is acceptable but the "shall not" form is the established legal idiom throughout Leviticus.

**V. 6 — "The LORD's food offerings" vs. "fire-offerings of the LORD."** EMET: "the fire-offerings of the LORD [ishei YHWH]." LOGOS: "the LORD's food offerings." PNEUMA: "the food offerings of the LORD." Consensus: "the LORD's food offerings" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The term *ishei* YHWH is technically "fire-offerings" (from *esh*, fire), but in context within the Holiness Code *ishei* functions broadly as "food offerings" (cf. the pairing with *lechem Eloheihem* in the same verse). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "food offerings" follows the established ESV/NASB convention for this context and avoids the awkward juxtaposition with "the bread of their God" in the same verse where two different food-terms are paired. The genitive "the LORD's food offerings" (LOGOS) is preferred over "the food offerings of the LORD" (PNEUMA) as more idiomatic English.

**V. 6 — "For it is they who present" vs. "they present."** EMET: "they present [hei maqrivim]." LOGOS: "for it is they who offer." PNEUMA: "Because they present." Consensus: "for it is they who present" — combining LOGOS's emphatic "for it is they who" (reflecting the pronoun *hem* — "they" standing emphatically) with "present" rather than LOGOS's "offer." The emphatic pronoun *hem* ("they," standing independently) in the MT warrants an emphatic English equivalent. "Present" is retained from the verbal root *qarav* (Hiphil = to bring near, offer/present); LOGOS uses "offer" which is also accurate. "Present" is preferred here since it has been used for the priestly technical action throughout Leviticus and the chapter uses both *qarav* and *hiqriv*.

**V. 7 — "Who is a prostitute" vs. "a whore."** EMET: "a woman who is a whore [ishah zonah]." LOGOS: "a woman who is a prostitute." PNEUMA: "a woman who is a prostitute." Consensus: "a woman who is a prostitute" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The terminology ruling for *zonah* established in the project is "prostitute" for the consensus text (project convention: "whore" in EMET's register is appropriate for literal translation but not the consensus norm). "Prostitute" is the standard formal rendering (ESV, NASB) and is used here and consistently throughout the chapter (vv. 7, 9, 14).

**V. 7 — "Who has been defiled" vs. "chalalah."** EMET: "one profaned [ve-chalalah — a 'desecrated/profaned woman']." LOGOS: "who has been defiled." PNEUMA: "who has been defiled." Consensus: "who has been defiled" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The term *chalalah* (a woman who has been "profaned/desecrated" in the priestly sense) is rendered "who has been defiled" rather than EMET's more literal "profaned woman." LOGOS's rendering is clear and accurate in legal English; the chalalah concept (a woman whose sacred/priestly household integrity has been violated) is captured in "defiled." Note: LOGOS in the term table renders chalalah as "woman who has been defiled" (vv. 7, 14) and the consensus follows this consistently.

**V. 7 — "They shall not marry" vs. "they shall not take."** EMET: "they shall not take [lo yiqqachu]." LOGOS: "They shall not marry." PNEUMA: "A priest must not marry." Consensus: "they shall not marry" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *laqach* (to take) in a marital context is naturally rendered "marry" in English; "take" is literal but sounds archaic without the Hebrew legal register. LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on "marry."

**V. 8 — "Consecrate" vs. "sanctify."** EMET: "you shall sanctify him [ve-qiddashto]." LOGOS: "You shall consecrate him." PNEUMA: "You must regard him as holy." Consensus: "You shall consecrate him" — LOGOS. Both "sanctify" and "consecrate" render *qiddashto* (Pi'el of q-d-sh); the Pi'el with a human object most commonly means "to consecrate" (to declare or treat as holy) rather than "sanctify" (to make holy in an internal sense). LOGOS's "consecrate" better fits the directive to the people: they are to treat the priest as consecrated. PNEUMA's paraphrase ("regard him as holy") captures the meaning but adds words beyond the Hebrew. Note: "who sanctifies you" in the same verse preserves "sanctifies" for the participial form *meqadishkhem* (LOGOS/EMET agree).

**V. 9 — "Profanes herself by going whoring" vs. "profanes herself by becoming a prostitute."** EMET: "when she profanes herself by going whoring [ki techal liznot]." LOGOS: "if she profanes herself by whoring." PNEUMA: "by becoming a prostitute." Consensus: "if she profanes herself by going whoring" — EMET/LOGOS adjusted. The MT verb *liznot* (Qal infinitive of *zanah* — to go whoring/play the prostitute) describes the action rather than a professional status. "Going whoring" (EMET/adjusted LOGOS) captures the verbal action. LOGOS drops "going" making it a noun rather than a verbal phrase; PNEUMA's "becoming a prostitute" is a professional status reading that is too static. EMET's "when" vs. LOGOS's "if" — the MT has *ki* which can be either; "if" (LOGOS) is the more natural legal conditional in this context.

**V. 9 — "She profanes her father."** All three agents agree on this phrase. Consensus: "she profanes her father" — universal agreement.

**V. 9 — "Shall be burned with fire."** EMET: "in fire she shall be burned." LOGOS: "she shall be burned with fire." PNEUMA: "She must be burned with fire." Consensus: "she shall be burned with fire" — LOGOS. Word order "burned with fire" follows LOGOS for natural English. "Shall be" is the standard legal modal throughout the chapter.

**V. 10 — "The high priest" vs. "the priest who is great/chief."** EMET: "the priest who is great [ha-kohen ha-gadol] among his brothers." LOGOS: "the priest who is chief among his brothers." PNEUMA: "The high priest." Consensus: "the high priest among his brothers" — terminology ruling specifies *kohen ha-gadol* = "high priest." PNEUMA simply uses "The high priest" without the fraternal qualifier; EMET/LOGOS preserve "among his brothers" (*me-echav*). The consensus preserves "among his brothers" as it is present in the MT (*ha-gadol me-echav*) and reflects the chapter's emphasis on the high priest as first within the priestly family, not categorically separate. The full phrase becomes "the high priest among his brothers" — a compromise that uses the established title but preserves the MT's relational qualifier.

**V. 10 — "Consecrated to wear" vs. "whose hand has been filled."** EMET: "who has been consecrated [ve-millea et-yado — lit. whose hand has been filled] to wear the garments." LOGOS: "who has been consecrated to wear the garments." PNEUMA: "who has been ordained to wear the priestly garments." Consensus: "who has been consecrated to wear the garments" — LOGOS. EMET preserves the literal idiom "whose hand has been filled" in brackets; the consensus text uses "consecrated" per LOGOS's decision. "Ordained" (PNEUMA) is theologically accurate but imports a later ecclesiastical vocabulary; "consecrated" is the more formal equivalent for the Hebrew *mille et-yado*. "The garments" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "the priestly garments" (PNEUMA) — the MT has simply *ha-begadim* ("the garments"); "priestly" is not in the Hebrew and is not added.

**V. 10 — "Shall not let the hair of his head hang loose" vs. "shall not uncover his head."** EMET: "his head he shall not uncover [lo yifra]." LOGOS: "shall not let the hair of his head hang loose." PNEUMA: "must not let his hair hang loose." Consensus: "shall not let the hair of his head hang loose" — LOGOS. The verb *para'* (Qal) in this context denotes the disheveled, unbound hair of mourning — "let hang loose" (LOGOS) is the standard ESV/NASB rendering (cf. Lev 10:6; Ezek 44:20) and is more informative than EMET's "uncover" (which obscures the specific mourning gesture). PNEUMA's "let his hair hang loose" is correct but drops "of his head" (*rosho*) which the MT includes.

**V. 10 — "Nor tear his garments."** EMET: "his garments he shall not tear [ve-et-begadav lo yifrom]." LOGOS: "nor tear his garments." PNEUMA: "or tear his clothes." Consensus: "nor tear his garments" — LOGOS. All three agents agree on the action; "garments" vs. "clothes" (PNEUMA) — "garments" is the more formal register consistent with the vestments-vocabulary of Leviticus.

**V. 11 — "Go in to any dead body" vs. "come to any dead person."** EMET: "to any dead person [ve-al kol-nefshot met] he shall not come." LOGOS: "He shall not go in to any dead body." PNEUMA: "He must not approach any dead body at all." Consensus: "He shall not go in to any dead body" — LOGOS. The MT has *ve-al kol-nafshot met lo yavo* ("and to all dead persons he shall not come/enter"). LOGOS's "go in to" captures the directional motion implicit in *bo* (to come/enter; here with directional *el* implied). EMET's "dead person" (nefesh ruling) vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "dead body" — the nefesh ruling specifies "dead person" in the corpse context; however, v. 11 uses *nafshot met* (lit. "dead souls/persons") while v. 1 uses simply *nefesh*. The ruling for nefesh in corpse context applies to both; however, "dead body" is well-established for this context in formal translation (ESV, NASB) and is retained from LOGOS since it is equally accurate and the distinction between "dead person" and "dead body" does not affect meaning here. Note: PNEUMA adds "at all" reflecting the LXX's *holos*; the MT has *kol* ("any/all") but not a separate emphatic adverb. "Any dead body" suffices.

**V. 11 — "Nor make himself unclean even for his father or for his mother."** EMET: "for his father and for his mother he shall not make himself unclean." LOGOS: "nor make himself unclean even for his father or for his mother." PNEUMA: "not even for his father or mother." Consensus: "nor make himself unclean even for his father or for his mother" — LOGOS. The "even" is warranted by the context (the most intimate relationships are explicitly included in the prohibition) though it is not a separate Hebrew word; the MT's force (*lo yittamma le-aviv u-le-immo*) conveys this absolute scope. LOGOS's "nor make himself unclean even for" preserves the legal formulation. PNEUMA's "not even for his father or mother" drops "make himself unclean" which is the key legal verb and should be retained.

**V. 12 — "Lest he profane" vs. "and he shall not profane."** EMET: "and he shall not profane [ve-lo yechalel] the sanctuary of his God." LOGOS: "lest he profane the sanctuary of his God." PNEUMA: "or he will desecrate the sanctuary of his God." Consensus: "lest he profane the sanctuary of his God" — LOGOS. The MT has *ve-lo yechalel*, a simple negative imperfect. However, in context after "he shall not go out of the sanctuary," the *ve-lo* clause expresses the purpose/result of the prohibition ("lest / so that he does not profane"). LOGOS's "lest he profane" captures this purpose-sense and flows more naturally. PNEUMA's "desecrate" for *challel* — the root is ch-l-l (profane); "profane" is the established project rendering throughout this chapter and is maintained.

**V. 12 — "The crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him."** EMET: "the crown of the anointing oil of his God [nezer shemen mishchat Elohav] is upon him." LOGOS: "the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him." PNEUMA: "For the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him." Consensus: "for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him" — EMET/LOGOS with PNEUMA's "for." The causal *ki* ("for/because") opening the clause in the MT is captured by PNEUMA's "For" which EMET and LOGOS both include ("for the crown..." — LOGOS actually has the "for" in v. 12 as "for the crown"). All three retain "crown" for *nezer* — consensus follows the agreed rendering. The full clause is identical across EMET and LOGOS; PNEUMA agrees. Consensus text: "for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him."

**V. 13 — "And he shall take a wife in her virginity."** EMET: "he — a woman in her virginity [be-vetuleha] he shall take as wife." LOGOS: "And he shall take a wife in her virginity." PNEUMA: "He must marry a woman who is a virgin." Consensus: "And he shall take a wife in her virginity" — LOGOS. The MT has *ve-hu be-vetuleha yiqqach ishah* — "and he — in her virginity he shall take a woman/wife." LOGOS's rendering is the closest natural English equivalent. "In her virginity" (LOGOS/EMET) preserves the Hebrew *be-vetuleha* as a state-description rather than simply "a virgin" (though both convey the same legal requirement). PNEUMA's "a woman who is a virgin" is accurate but loses the *be-vetuleha* ("in her virginity") phrasing. EMET's "he — ... as wife" is slightly awkward in English.

**V. 14 — "A widow" / four prohibited categories.** All three agents list: widow, divorced woman, defiled woman, prostitute. The order follows the MT: *almanah u-gerushah va-chalalah ve-zonah*. EMET: "widow / divorced woman / profaned woman / whore." LOGOS: "widow / divorced woman / woman who has been defiled / prostitute." PNEUMA: "widow / divorced woman / woman who has been defiled / prostitute." Consensus: "A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Profaned woman" (EMET) is the most literal for *chalalah* but is not the consensus term (ruling at v. 7 established "who has been defiled"). "Whore" (EMET) gives way to "prostitute" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) per the project consensus for this register.

**V. 14 — "These he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people."** EMET: "these he shall not take; but a virgin from his own people he shall take as wife." LOGOS: "these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people." PNEUMA: "only a virgin from his own people shall he take as his wife." Consensus: "these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people" — LOGOS. The distinction between "marry" (for the prohibited categories) and "take as his wife" (for the permitted virgin) follows LOGOS's elegant variation. PNEUMA omits the explicit "these he shall not marry" and goes directly to the positive command.

**V. 15 — "Profane his offspring" vs. "defile his children."** EMET: "he shall not profane his offspring [ve-lo yechalel zar'o]." LOGOS: "that he may not profane his offspring among his people." PNEUMA: "so that he does not defile his children among his people." Consensus: "that he may not profane his offspring among his people" — LOGOS. The root ch-l-l ("profane") is maintained over PNEUMA's "defile" (t-m-'). "Offspring" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "children" (PNEUMA) for *zar'o* ("his seed/offspring") in this dynastic context — the concern is with the priestly lineage extending forward, not merely with the high priest's immediate children.

**V. 16 — Opening formula.** EMET: "And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying." LOGOS: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying." PNEUMA: "The LORD spoke to Moses." Consensus: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying" — LOGOS. The *vav*-conjunction opening and the *lemor* ("saying") are both present in the MT and both preserved. PNEUMA drops "saying."

**V. 17 — "None of your offspring...who has a blemish."** EMET: "Any man from your offspring throughout their generations in whom there is a blemish shall not approach." LOGOS: "None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish may approach." PNEUMA: "no descendant of yours who has a physical defect may approach." Consensus: "None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish may approach" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "physical defect" for *mum* — the terminology ruling specifies *mum* = "blemish"; "physical defect" (PNEUMA throughout vv. 17–23) is an interpretive gloss that clarifies but departs from the established term. "Blemish" is the project standard (cf. LOGOS/EMET). "Throughout their generations" (*le-dorotam*) is preserved (LOGOS/EMET) against PNEUMA's omission.

**V. 18 — "Disfigured face" vs. "flat nose."** EMET: "one with a flat nose [charum]." LOGOS: "one who has a disfigured face." PNEUMA: "disfigured." Consensus: "one who has a disfigured face" — LOGOS. The term *charum* is uncertain; the LXX reads *kolobodaktulos* (shortened fingers) in one place and *koloborrin* (short-nosed) in another. "Disfigured face" (LOGOS/ESV) is the most defensible broad rendering for a term that may indicate any facial abnormality. EMET's "flat nose" is too specific for an uncertain term.

**V. 18 — "A limb too long" vs. "one with a limb too long."** EMET: "one with a limb too long [sarua]." LOGOS: "a limb too long." PNEUMA: "deformed." Consensus: "or a limb too long" — LOGOS. The MT uses *o sarua* ("or one who is stretched/extended"), most naturally referring to a disproportionately elongated limb. "A limb too long" (ESV/LOGOS) is the established formal rendering. PNEUMA's "deformed" is too vague and conflates *sarua* with the preceding term.

**V. 19 — "Broken foot or broken hand" vs. "injured foot or injured hand."** EMET: "a broken foot [shever regel] or a broken hand [shever yad]." LOGOS: "an injured foot or an injured hand." PNEUMA: "a broken foot or a broken hand." Consensus: "a broken foot or a broken hand" — EMET/PNEUMA. The MT has *shever regel o shever yad* — literally "a break of foot or a break of hand." *Shever* means "break/fracture" (from *shavar*, to break). "Broken" (EMET/PNEUMA) is the more literal and precise rendering; LOGOS's "injured" is more general and less specific to *shever*.

**V. 20 — "Hunchback" / "dwarf."** EMET: "a hunchback [gibben] or a dwarf [daq]." LOGOS: "a hunchback or a dwarf." PNEUMA: "hunchbacked or stunted." Consensus: "a hunchback or a dwarf" — EMET/LOGOS. "Hunchback" for *gibben* (well-attested; LXX *kyrtos*, "bent/hump-backed") — all agree. "Dwarf" for *daq* (LOGOS/EMET) vs. "stunted" (PNEUMA) — "dwarf" is the standard formal rendering (ESV, NASB); PNEUMA's "stunted" implies a different physical condition (growth failure/wasting) and is less conventional.

**V. 20 — "A defect in his eye" vs. "a defect in his sight."** EMET: "one with a defect in his eye [o tevelul be-eino]." LOGOS: "a man with a defect in his sight." PNEUMA: "an eye defect." Consensus: "a man with a defect in his eye" — EMET. The MT has *tevelul be-eino* — "a confusion/spot in his eye" — referring to the organ (*eino*, "his eye"), not his faculty of sight (*re'iyah*). EMET's "in his eye" is closer to the Hebrew anatomical reference. PNEUMA's shortening to "an eye defect" loses the personal construction ("in his eye"). LOGOS's "in his sight" shifts from the organ to the function.

**V. 20 — "Itching disease" vs. "festering sore" vs. "festering sores."** EMET: "a festering sore [garav]." LOGOS: "an itching disease." PNEUMA: "festering sores." Consensus: "an itching disease" — LOGOS. The term *garav* is uncertain; Talmudic tradition associates it with a weeping/oozing skin lesion; the LXX uses *psora agria* ("wild/severe mange"). "Itching disease" (LOGOS/ESV) is the more cautious and widely-adopted formal rendering. EMET's "festering sore" is one interpretation; PNEUMA's "festering sores" is a slight expansion.

**V. 20 — "Scabs" for yalefet.** EMET: "scabs [yalefet]." LOGOS: "scabs." PNEUMA omits this term separately (absorbed into "festering sores"). Consensus: "or scabs" — EMET/LOGOS. Both preserve the term; PNEUMA's omission of a distinct term for *yalefet* is not followed.

**V. 20 — "Crushed testicles" for meroa ashekh.** EMET: "crushed testicles [meroa ashekh]." LOGOS: "crushed testicles." PNEUMA: "damaged testicles." Consensus: "or crushed testicles" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT has *meroa ashekh* — literally "crushed/bruised of testicle." "Crushed testicles" (EMET/LOGOS) renders the MT accurately; PNEUMA's "damaged" is softer and less precise for *meroa* (from *rua*, to crush/shatter).

**V. 21 — "Come near to offer the LORD's food offerings."** EMET: "he shall not draw near to present the fire-offerings of the LORD." LOGOS: "shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings." PNEUMA: "may approach to offer the food offerings of the LORD." Consensus: "shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings" — LOGOS. Maintains "food offerings" from the v. 6 ruling. "Come near" vs. "draw near" (EMET) — both render *nagas* (draw near/come near); LOGOS/PNEUMA's "come near" is slightly more natural.

**V. 22 — "Most holy things and the holy things."** EMET: "from the most holy things and from the holy things — he may eat." LOGOS: "the bread of his God, both the most holy and the holy things." PNEUMA: "the most holy food of his God, as well as the other holy food." Consensus: "He may eat the bread of his God, both the most holy things and the holy things" — adjusting LOGOS. LOGOS has "He may eat the bread of his God, both the most holy and the holy things" — inserting "things" after "most holy" makes parallel with "holy things" more explicit. PNEUMA's "other holy food" for *ha-qedashim* is a paraphrase. "Things" is present in EMET explicitly (*mi-qodesh ha-qodashim u-min-ha-qedashim*) and implied in LOGOS.

**V. 23 — "Shall not go in to the veil" vs. "shall not come near the veil."** EMET: "to the veil [el-ha-parokhet] he shall not come." LOGOS: "he shall not go through the veil." PNEUMA: "he must not go near the veil." Consensus: "he shall not go in to the veil" — adjusted from EMET/LOGOS. The MT has *el-ha-parokhet lo yavo* — "to the veil he shall not come/enter." LOGOS's "go through the veil" imports the idea of passing through it, which is not in the Hebrew; the prohibition is against approaching/entering the veil's space. EMET's "come" is literal; "go in to" captures the spatial approach. PNEUMA's "go near" is too weak — *bo* (come/enter) implies a stronger proximate approach than "go near."

**V. 23 — "Come near to the altar" vs. "approach the altar."** EMET: "to the altar he shall not draw near." LOGOS: "or approach the altar." PNEUMA: "or approach the altar." Consensus: "or come near to the altar" — consistent with "shall not go in to the veil...or come near to the altar." The MT has *ve-el-ha-mizbeach lo yiggash* ("and to the altar he shall not draw near/approach"). "Come near" is used for consistency with the veil clause.

**V. 23 — "My sanctuaries" vs. "my sanctuary."** EMET: "my sanctuaries [et-miqdashay]." LOGOS: "my sanctuaries." PNEUMA: "my holy places." Consensus: "my sanctuaries" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT plural *miqdashay* ("my sanctuaries") is preserved. PNEUMA's "my holy places" is accurate but loses the root *miqdash* which links to the earlier uses of *miqdash* (sanctuary) in the chapter (vv. 12, 23).

**V. 23 — "Who sanctifies them."** EMET: "I am the LORD who sanctifies them [meqadsham]." LOGOS: "for I am the LORD who sanctifies them." PNEUMA: "I am the LORD who makes them holy." Consensus: "for I am the LORD who sanctifies them" — LOGOS. The *ki* ("for/because") before "I am the LORD" in the MT is preserved with LOGOS's "for." PNEUMA's "makes them holy" is a paraphrase of the participial *meqadsham*; "sanctifies them" (EMET/LOGOS) is the standard formal rendering for this formula throughout the Holiness Code.

**V. 24 — "Sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel" vs. "Israelites."** EMET: "all the sons of Yisra'el." LOGOS: "all the people of Israel." PNEUMA: "all the Israelites." Consensus: "all the sons of Israel" — EMET's rendering (minus the transliteration). The terminology ruling specifies *bney yisrael* = "sons of Israel." LOGOS's "people of Israel" has been used at Lev 20:24; but the project ruling for *bney yisrael* is "sons of Israel" as the consistent formal rendering throughout the consensus texts (cf. Lev 20 Council Notes: "bney yisrael = 'sons of Israel'"). PNEUMA's "Israelites" is too colloquial for the legal closing formula.

**V. 24 — "And Moses spoke" vs. "So Moses spoke."** EMET: "And Moshe spoke [vay-daber Moshe]." LOGOS: "So Moses spoke." PNEUMA: "So Moses told all this to." Consensus: "And Moses spoke" — EMET/adjusted. The MT has *vay-daber Moshe* — the *vav*-consecutive imperfect, meaning "and Moses spoke." LOGOS's "So" is an interpretive connector (summary); EMET's "And" is more literal and preserves the *vav* consecutive. PNEUMA adds "all this" which is not in the Hebrew.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the graduated holiness structure:** Leviticus 21 operates on a three-tiered holiness map that mirrors the spatial zones of the Sinai sanctuary: ordinary Israelite → ordinary priest (vv. 1–9) → high priest (vv. 10–15) → blemished priest (vv. 16–24). Each tier intensifies the restrictions of the one before. The ordinary priest may defile himself for six named relatives; the high priest may defile himself for none. The ordinary priest may marry any eligible woman from the permitted pool; the high priest may marry only a virgin from his own people. The blemished priest may neither offer nor approach the altar or veil, but retains full priestly eating-rights. The chapter's governing concern is not cruelty but correspondence: the closer one stands to the divine presence, the more the whole person — body, household, relationships — must embody the wholeness that divine presence demands.

**On *mum* and the sacrifice-parallel:** The blemish law of vv. 17–24 has a deliberate structural parallel with the animal-offering requirements of Leviticus 22:17–25, where sacrificial animals must also be "without blemish" (*tamim*, without *mum*). The priest who presents the offering must meet the same standard as the offering he presents. Both the priest's body and the animal's body represent Israel before God; both must be complete. The blemished priest is not expelled from the covenant or from the priestly table — he retains full eating-rights (v. 22). His restriction is functional (the specific act of altar-service and approach to the veil) not ontological (his priestly identity and standing).

**On *nezer* (v. 12):** The term *nezer* (crown/consecration) appears in the priestly context as a designation for the consecrating mark of the high priest's office — the anointing oil itself functions as a crown. The same word describes the Nazirite's consecrated status in Numbers 6, creating a typological link between the high priest's lifelong consecration and the Nazirite's temporary dedication. Both are prohibited from corpse-contact; both wear a *nezer*. The LXX at v. 12 renders *nezer* as *hagiasma* ("holy thing") rather than "crown," losing the coronation-consecration semantic link present in the Hebrew.

**On *chalalah* (vv. 7, 14):** The *chalalah* ("woman who has been defiled/profaned," from *challal*) is a specific legal category in priestly law: a woman who has lost the priestly household's consecrated integrity, either through illicit sexual relations or through birth from a disqualified priestly union. The word shares its root with *challel* (to profane), creating an internal root-play throughout the chapter between the acts of profanation forbidden to the priest and the status of the profaned woman he is forbidden to marry. This root-play is present in the Hebrew but cannot be fully captured in English; it is noted here for the scholarly record.

**On the LXX divergence at v. 14 (*be-vetuleha*):** The LXX of v. 14 renders *be-vetuleha* ("in her virginity") as *en to genei autou* ("in his own clan/lineage"), apparently reading the phrase as specifying endogamy (marriage within priestly kin) rather than virginal status. This is a significant divergence from the MT: the MT's explicit requirement that the high priest marry a *betulah* (virgin) is replaced in the LXX with a lineage restriction. The consensus follows the MT.

**On the LXX divergence at v. 18 (*sarua*):** The LXX renders *sarua* ("a limb too long") as *ototomos* or similar ("one with a cut ear"), a strikingly different reading that may reflect a different Vorlage or a lexical confusion. The MT's "a limb too long" is followed in the consensus.

**On the blemish list's uncertain terms (vv. 18–20):** Several terms in the blemish catalogue remain lexically uncertain: *charum* (probably a nasal or facial abnormality, not specifically "flat nose" or "slit nose"); *daq* (probably small stature/dwarfism, though possibly emaciation); *tevelul be-eino* (probably an ocular opacity or spot — cataract, leucoma — though the exact medical identification is uncertain); *garav* (a skin condition, likely chronic and weeping); *yalefet* (another skin condition; LXX *leichen*, "lichen/scaly skin disease"). All uncertain terms are rendered with the most widely attested formal English equivalent.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 21:1–24. All 24 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_021.md`.*
